headline: GRAINS GRIND HIGHER, LIVESTOCK HOLDS STEADY
subheadline: Corn leads the complex up 1.3% as winter wheat crop hits 61-year low.
lead: Corn closed $4.17, up 5¼ cents and leading the grain complex higher for a second straight session. USDA's winter wheat crop estimate dropped to its smallest since 1965, adding supply tightness to a market that's been grinding higher all week. The funds aren't chasing yet-open interest stayed flat-but the price action says somebody's accumulating on this five-day slide turned bounce.
the_takeaway: Grain complex breaking out of its week-long slide on tightening supply.
teaser: Winter wheat hits 61-year production low as grains reverse the week's decline.

one_number.unit: years since winter wheat production was this low
one_number.context: USDA cut winter wheat outlook another 2% Thursday, marking the smallest crop since 1965. Plains drought carved yield estimates deeper than expected. The move adds structural tightness to wheat fundamentals just as corn starts showing seasonal strength into pollination season.

yesterdays_call.summary: Funds getting defensive ahead of WASDE, Brazil supply weighing on outlook.
yesterdays_call.note: Grains reversed higher instead of continuing the slide, winter wheat supply tightness changed the story.

sections.title: GRAINS REVERSE HIGHER
sections.body: Corn gained 5¼ cents to $4.17, leading the complex out of this week's slide. Soybeans added a nickel to $11.19, wheat firmed 4¼ cents to $5.94. **The winter wheat story is the catalyst**: USDA cut the crop estimate another 2%, putting 2026 production at the smallest since 1965. Plains drought cut deeper than the market priced. Fund positioning stayed neutral-no big money chasing the move yet-but the accumulation pattern is building. Dec corn at $4.43 tells you the carry's still working, old crop into new crop isn't pricing weather premium.
sections.catalyst: USDA winter wheat crop estimate cut to smallest since 1965
sections.bottom_line: Supply tightness overrode fund defensiveness, grains found their footing.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: reversed the Brazil supply drag, winter wheat tightness reset the story.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK HOLDS GROUND
sections.body: Live cattle eased 40 cents to $241.65, feeders dropped $1.40 to $358.12, but the selling stayed orderly. **Seventh screwworm case confirmed** brought New Mexico into the outbreak zone, but the market's already priced the processing disruption risk. Hogs slipped 75 cents to $95.00 on quiet trade. The cattle complex isn't breaking down, it's just not finding new buyers at these levels. Screwworm containment efforts ramped up with sterile fly releases, and Secretary Rollins made the Texas trip to show federal priority.
sections.bottom_line: Disease spread contained in price, cattle holding support levels.

outside_the_pit.title: University of Florida gets $9.5M for cattle pest research
outside_the_pit.body: The funding targets mosquito and fly-borne disease prevention, coming as screwworm cases climb to seven across Texas and New Mexico. Research money flows faster when the threat lands on ranchers' doorsteps.

outside_the_pit.title: Sen. Marshall drops co-sponsorship of Prop 12 challenge bill
outside_the_pit.body: The Kansas Republican withdrew from the Save Our Bacon Act that would negate California's hog housing requirements. Political calculus shifting as implementation costs get clearer for producers.

outside_the_pit.title: EPA faces lawsuit challenge to RFS Set 2 rule
outside_the_pit.body: American Fuel & Petrochemical Manufacturers filed suit claiming record-high compliance costs for biofuel blending mandates. RFS regulations keep getting costlier to implement, squeezing refinery margins that eventually flow back to ethanol demand.

spread_to_watch.label: July wheat / November soybeans ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: Wheat's winter crop tightness is closing the gap with beans faster than usual for this time of year. When this ratio drops below 0.50, wheat typically finds fresh money rotating out of other grains. The compression says somebody thinks wheat's the better drought play heading into summer.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming on ethanol demand pickup
basis.body: **Corn basis east of the Mississippi is tightening** as ethanol plants come back online after maintenance season. Indiana and Ohio elevators reporting stronger local demand. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern. Soybean basis holding steady, processors working through old-crop inventory before new-crop concerns build.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain complex break out of its five-day slide this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: The answer is yes. Grains reversed the week's losses on winter wheat supply tightness, corn leading the bounce. The slide broke Thursday on USDA production cuts.

the_more_you_know.title: Why 61-year winter wheat lows matter more than the headline
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 61-year winter wheat production low isn't just a Plains drought story-it's a structural shift. Winter wheat used to be the backstop when spring crops got tight. Now it's spring wheat and corn carrying that role. The 1965 comparison matters because that was the last time wheat acres contracted this hard without a federal program driving it. Today's contraction is purely economic: farmers choosing corn and beans over wheat because the margins work better. When the backstop crop becomes the problem crop, the whole rotation math changes.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn planting above 75% confirms Belt caught up from May rains
Weekly export sales: wheat above 500K MT tests if winter crop tightness draws buying
Screwworm sterile fly release results in affected Texas counties

daily_quote.text: A journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.

source_summary: USDA reports, CME futures, industry news
